did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for the classification of skin lesions. It utilizes the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images and reports achieving a training accuracy of 100% and a validation accuracy of 98.32%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom CNN framework for skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methodology (data splitting, oversampling, architecture), hardware specifications, and reports new quantitative results (100% training accuracy, 98.32% validation accuracy). Although it includes educational components, it is published in the 2021 International Conference on Industrial Electronics Research and Applications (ICIERA) and reports original technical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Anatomization for Classification Skin Lesion Using Custom CNN Framework
journal: 2021 International Conference on Industrial Electronics Research and Applications (ICIERA)
brief_summary: This study presents a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework for the classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images. The methodology includes data preprocessing, image resizing to 28x28 pixels, oversampling to balance class distribution, and splitting the data into training (80%), testing (10%), and validation (10%) sets. The primary evaluation approach involved training the custom CNN model and assessing its performance through accuracy metrics. The key result reported was a training accuracy of 100% and a validation accuracy of 98.32%. The work aims to provide an accessible, open-source tool for understanding CNN operations in dermatology without requiring complex computational skills.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Table I lists 'Mel' with 4 images in the subset shown, and the text notes melanoma is the 'worst form of skin cancer'.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'bcc' with 1 image in the subset shown. Text mentions it as a significant diagnostic group.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'Akiec' with 0 images in the subset shown. Text groups it under significant diagnostic groups.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'bkl' with 2 images in the subset shown. Text describes it as benign keratosis-like lesions.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'df' with 3 images in the subset shown.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'vasc' with 6 images in the subset shown. Text lists specific types like angiokeratomas and angiomas.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. Table I lists 'nv' with 5 images in the subset shown. Text refers to benign lesions known as moles.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, etc.) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classification of skin lesions' using a custom CNN framework. It reports classification metrics such as training accuracy (100%) and validation accuracy (98.32%). The target classes are discrete categories from the HAM10000 dataset.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10000 training pictures)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images collected from various populations to address the lack of variety and limited size of available datasets for automatic identification of pigmented skin lesions. The dataset contains images captured using various modalities, with lesions verified by histopathology, follow-up investigation, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy. It was obtained from Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN framework. The dataset was split into a training set (80%), testing set (10%), and validation set (10%). Oversampling was applied to balance the class distribution.
use.validation: Used for validation (10% of the split data) to assess model performance, achieving a validation accuracy of 98.32%.
use.testing: Used for testing (10% of the split data) to evaluate the model.
use.other: The paper notes that the data is taken from the 'ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' grand challenge datasets.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To summarize predictions and calculate accuracy by counting how many times each class was correctly classified.
To balance the class distribution in the dataset, addressing the uneven number of photos representing various types of lesions.
To partition the dataset into training (80%), testing (10%), and validation (10%) sets to evaluate model performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics (training accuracy of 100% and validation accuracy of 98.32%) and a confusion matrix.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports a training accuracy of 100% and a validation accuracy of 98.32%. The specific loss values are provided in Table II. The dataset was split into 80% training, 10% testing, and 10% validation. Oversampling was used to balance class distribution.

architecture_details.implementation_details: Built using the Keras Python module. The model processes images resized to 28x28 pixels. The architecture involves convolutional layers (implied by 'convolution moves on to the next pixel') and a final classification layer that selects the class with the highest logit value.

training_details.hardware: Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-5820K CPU (3.30 GHz), 64 GB RAM, Titan X Pascal GPU (12 GB RAM)

classification_or_output_head.description: Selects the class with the highest logit value as the prediction.

preprocessing_techniques: Data splitting into training (80%), testing (10%), and validation (10%) sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to balance the class distribution in the HAM10000 dataset, addressing the imbalance where certain lesion types had fewer images than others. No specific probabilistic parameters or geometric/color transform details were reported for this technique.

conclusions: The custom CNN framework achieved a training accuracy of 100% and a validation accuracy of 98.32% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The study demonstrates that deep learning architectures can provide impressive results for image categorization in dermatology, potentially aiding in the early detection of melanoma.

Providing hands-on, open-source educational activities for CNNs can bridge the knowledge gap for non-experts and students without strong mathematical backgrounds, allowing them to understand algorithmic principles intuitively.

The proposed method aims to make deep learning tools accessible to a larger population by reducing technical and technological obstacles, enabling the use of cloud-based platforms like Google Colab and GitHub without complex software installation.

Future work includes broadening the training scope to incorporate more images and courses, such as different types and stages of melanoma skin cancers, and enhancing the retrieval phase by including new classes in case representation.
important_remarks: The paper is primarily an educational tutorial for students rather than a rigorous clinical study, aiming to bridge the knowledge gap for non-experts.
The custom CNN architecture uses a very small input size of 28x28 pixels, which may discard critical dermatological features compared to standard high-resolution inputs.

The study reports a training accuracy of 100% and validation accuracy of 98.32%, which are unusually high for this complex multi-class problem and may indicate overfitting or data leakage, although oversampling was used.
The dataset (HAM10000) is imbalanced; the authors used oversampling to balance classes, but the specific technique (e.g., SMOTE, random oversampling) is not detailed.
The dataset includes images verified by histopathology, follow-up, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy, introducing potential label noise due to varying ground truth reliability.
The paper does not report standard clinical metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, or AUC for the proposed custom CNN, limiting its clinical interpretability.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, relying solely on the internal split of the HAM10000 dataset.

The hardware used (Titan X Pascal) and software environment (Google Colab/GitHub) suggest the work is designed for accessibility and reproducibility by students rather than high-performance clinical deployment.
